questions.question: What is the correct sequence for setting up email infrastructure?

options.text: Buy domain, connect to Instantly, start sending
Buy domains (plural), configure DNS, verify with tools, warmup 30 days, monitor continuously, then start sending
Start sending, then fix any issues that come up
Buy one domain, send 100 emails to test, then scale

questions.explanation: The correct sequence is: buy multiple domains, configure DNS correctly (SPF/DKIM/DMARC), verify with multiple testing tools, warmup for 30 days, set up continuous monitoring, and only then start cold sending. This produces 80% inbox placement vs 15% with the shortcut approach.

questions.question: What did a technical founder waste $10,000 on before realizing the problem?

options.text: Apollo credits and Instantly subscriptions — sending to 5,000 leads and getting 3 replies because emails were going to spam with zero monitoring

questions.explanation: The founder spent $10,000 on Apollo credits and Instantly subscriptions, burned through 5,000 leads, and got only 3 replies. The problem wasn't the offer or copy — it was infrastructure he never validated. Without monitoring, he had no way to know his emails were landing in spam.

questions.question: What is the difference between 80% inbox placement and 15% inbox placement?

options.text: Following the correct infrastructure setup sequence (domains, DNS, verification, warmup, monitoring) vs skipping straight to sending

questions.explanation: The difference between 80% and 15% inbox placement is entirely about following the correct setup sequence. Both approaches can use identical tools and identical copy — the founders who validate infrastructure before sending achieve 80%, while those who skip to sending get 15%.

questions.question: The deliverability checklist is a one-time setup — once you've completed all steps, you never need to revisit it.
questions.explanation: False. The checklist includes ongoing monitoring requirements: continuous tracking of inbox placement, domain reputation, and complaint rates. Deliverability is a living system that requires regular attention. Reputation can change weekly based on sending patterns, complaint rates, and ISP policy updates.

questions.question: Rate your current email infrastructure on a scale of 1-10 for completeness. Which checklist items have you completed, and which are missing? What is the single most impactful step you could take this week to improve your deliverability?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response provides an honest self-assessment, identifies specific completed and missing checklist items (DNS, warmup, monitoring, domain rotation), and proposes a concrete, actionable next step. Look for understanding that infrastructure validation must happen before scale.
